text:
The assumption that technology operates as a neutral tool — indifferent to the purposes of its users — has persisted since the industrial revolution, and it remains one of the most consequential misconceptions of the modern era. Tools are designed by people with intentions, deployed within systems that have incentives, and adopted by populations that have vulnerabilities. To pretend otherwise is to surrender any possibility of shaping how technology affects the society that produces it.

Consider the smartphone. When Steve Jobs introduced the iPhone in 2007, he framed it as three devices in one: a phone, a music player, and an internet communicator. What he did not mention — what perhaps no one fully understood at the time — was that it would also become a surveillance device, an advertising platform, a gambling machine, and a social anxiety generator. None of these functions are bugs. They are features, meticulously designed by teams of engineers who understood human psychology well enough to exploit it at scale. The notification badge is red because red triggers urgency. The feed refreshes with a slot-machine pull because variable reinforcement schedules are the most effective way to create compulsive behavior. These are not accidents.

The counterargument is familiar and not without merit: technology is what you make of it. The same smartphone that enables doom-scrolling also enables a paramedic to access medical records in an emergency, a refugee to navigate unfamiliar terrain, a student in a rural town to attend a lecture at a world-class university. This is true, and it is insufficient. The fact that a tool can be used well does not absolve its designers of responsibility for the ways it is most commonly used. A casino that also houses a library is still primarily a casino if ninety percent of its visitors are there to gamble.

What makes the neutrality myth particularly dangerous is that it forecloses the most important conversation: what do we want these technologies to do? If a platform's primary function is to maximize engagement, and if engagement is most efficiently maximized through outrage, misinformation, and social comparison, then the platform is not neutral. It has a bias toward dysfunction. Acknowledging this bias is the first step toward demanding something better. Not a rejection of technology — that ship sailed long before any of us were born — but an insistence that the tools we build reflect the values we claim to hold.

The path forward requires a willingness to abandon comfortable fictions. Technology is not neutral. Markets are not self-correcting. Users are not fully rational agents making informed choices in a transparent marketplace. Once we accept these realities, we can begin the more productive work of designing systems that account for human limitations rather than exploiting them. This is not anti-technology sentiment. It is the most pro-technology stance possible: the belief that we can build tools worthy of the species that created them.
